points: The Highest Common Factor (HCF) or Greatest Common Divisor (GCD) is the greatest number that divides two or more numbers without leaving a remainder. It can be found by listing common factors or using prime factorisation.

Prime factorisation is the process of expressing a number as a product of prime numbers. To find HCF using prime factorisation, identify common prime factors and multiply the lowest powers of these factors.
Real-world applications of HCF include finding the largest tile size for flooring without cutting, determining maximum bag sizes for packing items equally, and optimizing distribution of resources.
The Lowest Common Multiple (LCM) is the smallest positive number that is divisible by two or more numbers. It can be found by listing common multiples or using prime factorisation.
To find LCM using prime factorisation, identify all prime factors from both numbers and multiply the highest powers of these factors. This method is efficient even for large numbers.
Real-world applications of LCM include scheduling recurring events (e.g., determining when activities coincide), planning production cycles, and solving problems involving periodic intervals.
The fundamental property states: HCF(a, b) × LCM(a, b) = a × b. This relationship connects HCF and LCM and is useful for calculations and verification.
Special patterns exist for HCF: consecutive numbers have HCF = 1; if one number is a multiple of another, HCF = the smaller number; co-prime numbers (HCF = 1) have LCM = their product.

Mathematical thinking involves making conjectures (unproven claims from observed patterns) and generalisations (proven statements). Testing and proving relationships between HCF and LCM strengthens problem-solving skills.
